description: Pothos (Epipremnum aureum), also called Devil’s Ivy, is one of the most common houseplants and a frequent cause of plant-related toxicity in cats. As a member of the Araceae family, it contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals (raphides) throughout its leaves and stems. Chewing the plant causes immediate intense oral pain and irritation.
name: Pothos / Devil’s Ivy
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: insoluble calcium oxalate raphides; sap also contains proteolytic enzymes that intensify the reaction
Mechanism: crystals are forcefully ejected into the oral mucosa when plant cells rupture, causing mechanical burning and swelling
Symptoms: sudden oral pain, intense drooling, pawing at mouth, difficulty swallowing, swollen lips and tongue, vomiting
The trailing vines are very accessible to cats, especially when draped over furniture or shelves
Rinse the mouth with water; seek veterinary care if swallowing is impaired or symptoms do not subside

symptoms.name: Intense burning, irritation, and pain of the mouth, lips, and tongue
Swelling of the mouth and tongue, accompanied by difficulty swallowing (dysphagia)
